sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.

You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 170, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, SPACE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: SPACE converted the white top-right frame back to dark gray (G)! W bar at col 43 (RIGHT). Charcoal frame at cols 36-39 unchanged. The SPACE shot toggles frame colors: charcoal -> white -> dark gray. Need to get W bar to LEFT to shoot charcoal frame. Try shooting dark gray frame again to reset it, which might switch W bar.
- Plan: First, inspect the board segmentation to understand all objects, their positions, colors, and relationships. Then probe with movement actions to understand the game mechanics.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 169 actions; a strong score needs about 71 or fewer. You are 2.4x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.content: The board changed. The animation_changed_bbox is [24, 44, 27, 47], which is the top-right frame region. Let me check the current state.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check current state
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['N', 'W', 'b', 'c', 'g', 'G']:
        rows = [p[0] for p in n['boundary']]
        cols = [p[1] for p in n['boundary']]
        print(f"{n['color']}: rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}")

# Check ascii around player and frames
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("\nRegion (rows 22-32, cols 34-56):")
for i in range(22, 32):
    print(f"{i:2d}: {ascii_lines[i][34:56]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The dark gray frame (G) at cols 44-47 converted back to white (W)! So the sequence is: charcoal -> white -> dark gray -> white. Or maybe it's: charcoal -> white -> dark gray -> charcoal -> white -> ...
Wait, the initial state was charcoal (c). After shooting, it became white (W). After shooting again, it became dark gray (G). After shooting again, it became white (W) again.
So the sequence might be: charcoal -> white -> dark gray -> white -> charcoal -> white -> dark gray -> ...
Or maybe it's: charcoal -> white -> dark gray -> white -> dark gray -> ... (cycling between white and dark gray after the first shot).
Actually, looking at the ascii:
Row 24: `wwccccNNNWWWWWwwwwwwww`
- cccc at cols 36-39: charcoal frame (unchanged)
- NNN at cols 40-42: player (unchanged)
- WWWW at cols 43-47: W bar + white frame (merged)
So the top-right frame is white again. The W bar is at cols 43-47 (merged with the white frame).
Now I'm back to the state where the top-right frame is white. The charcoal frame at cols 36-39 is still charcoal.
I need to convert the charcoal frame to white. To do that, I need the W bar to point LEFT. But the W bar is on the RIGHT side.
Let me think about this differently. Maybe I can move the player to a position where the charcoal frame is on the RIGHT side of the player. Then the W bar (pointing RIGHT) would hit the charcoal frame.
The charcoal frame is at cols 36-39. The player is at cols 40-42. To get the charcoal frame on the RIGHT side of the player, I need to move the player to cols 32-34 (LEFT of the charcoal frame). But the player is blocked moving LEFT by the charcoal frame.
Wait, I can't move the player through the charcoal frame. So I can't get the player to the LEFT of the charcoal frame.
Hmm, let me think about this problem differently. The charcoal frame is at cols 36-39, and the player is at cols 40-42. The player is adjacent to the charcoal frame on the RIGHT side.
Maybe I can move the player UP or DOWN to get around the charcoal frame. The charcoal frame is at rows 24-27. If I move the player UP to rows 20-23, I can then move LEFT past the charcoal frame, and then move DOWN to get to the LEFT side of the charcoal frame.
Let me try moving UP.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content: World model: Top-right frame is white again (W). Charcoal frame at cols 36-39 unchanged. W bar on RIGHT. Need to get W bar to LEFT to shoot charcoal frame. Can't move LEFT through charcoal frame. Try moving UP to go around the charcoal frame.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Try moving UP to go around the charcoal frame
action(['UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
